speaker: interviewer
text: Interviewer-- James Alexander Bond. New York City, USA.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in summation, perhaps tolerance is the one attitude, I believe, that sums up best what I have been through and what I've gotten out of this experience. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. Who's in it, and any other information you want to give about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this is a photograph, the only one I have left of the family still in-- living a normal life. From left to right, that's me. The picture was taken in Paris in 1939, shortly after my bar-- bar mitzvah. Me, my father, little brother, Harry, who was six years younger, and my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, these pictures were taken in 1943 while I was hidden. That's me. I was hidden in the orphanage, and I was given one of the uniforms that all the children wear, or wore, at the time. With this uniform, once I had it, I-- I-- I ventured outside of the orphanage with all the other kids on organized outings in the area, and occasionally we went to see a movie. But that's the uniform of the orphanage. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There going upstairs to hide under the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, oh, didn't realize. This is a picture of my friend, Willy. Willy Hartmayer, the one who was caught with me at the cinema, but who managed to escape while still in the cinema by running upstairs and hiding under the stashed seats that had been removed from the floor. And he is-- also, you see him here in the uniform with the cape and the hat, the uniform of the orphanage, Madonnina Del Grappa in Rifredi, Florence. He survived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, on the left here is a picture of Don Julio Fascimeni, who by that time was already a monsignor. He was the head of the orphanage in Rifredi, where I was hidden. A very frail man, shaken by Parkinson's disease. Always with a handkerchief tucked between the buttons of his cassock so that he could reach it easily.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is a picture taken a little later, in 1953. On my way to America, I stopped in Florence to revisit him. And that's me in the back. And this was his assistant, Don [? Melo. ?] Of all the people at the orphanage, only three people knew that we were Jews. These two priests and one more, Signor Luigi. I don't know his-- his full name. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Can I still say something? Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: This person was so revered all through Florence, I wouldn't be surprised if, in time, he's officially declared a saint.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to go back to the first picture and take a better shot. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] Interesting, this picture is. So we have speed. You can say something.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this picture, again, was taken in 1953. On the same voyage from Israel to New York, I stopped off in Florence. First you saw the picture of Monsignor Julio Fascimeni And I now went north to-- to revisit my Catholic friends there. This was taken in the courtyard of the Collegio [? Pio ?] [? Decimo ?] where Don Giovanni was by then teaching history and philosophy, and so was Don-- Don Angelo. So from left to-- here, this one here is Don Giovanni Semioni. This one here is Don Angelo De La Torre. But they enlisted the help of two more friends, Don Bartelusi and Don de Sopi. All of this is what I call the Catholic Conspiracy. And this was one of the many times we got together again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's you in the middle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Should I mention that? This non-Catholic priest in the middle is me, Louis Goldman. Jewish refugee boy. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, here you see Don Giovanni Semioni. And here he-- he is with the immediate members of those that he hid. Now, these by no means, are all the people he took care of, but specifically my brother Harry, my mother, my cousin, Sylvia, her mother, my Aunt Sonia, and a girl by the name of Renee who now lives in-- married with children who lives in Paris. This girl is now a mother, married, lives in New-- New Jersey. Aunt Sonia and my mother died a few years ago. And my brother's the one who-- who-- who-- died during the War of Liberation, 1948, in-- in-- in Israel, ironically, two hours before the ceasefire. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture here was taken a few weeks after the liberation of Treviso, which was not liberated before the last of the war, May 8, 1945. This was taken a few weeks later. Don Giovanni Semioni, my mother, my brother, Harry, my cousin, Sylvia, her mother, Aunt Sonia, and an orphan girl, Renee, who now lives in Paris; married, with a child, too. My brother was, I mentioned in the tape, was killed in 1948 in the War of Liberation, ironically, two hours before the cease-- ceasefire. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's the group.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of the actual cave where Don Giovanni hid the twelve women and girls that were hidden in the convent. And this cave is in the garden of the convent that had been, until then used to-- as a storage place for agricultural implements and bags of fertilizer, and what else they needed for their gardens. It was all removed and the women would be sitting and sleeping on these two ledges here. Now you see some tomatoes or other vegetables drying there, but at the time there was nothing. And this was the access door up a few steps to the garden. The door was blocked, of course, in case Germans came, from the outside. And only the nuns knew, and came with food, knocking on the door with a special signal so the women inside would-- would-- would know that it's safe to open the door. So again, this is the cave of-- in the garden of the convent of Varlungo in Florence.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no? The two pictures at the bottom were taken when the priests, after they were given the medal of the Righteous Among the Nations. That ceremony took place in-- in Italy at the Italian-- at the Israeli consulate in Rome. But later, then, following that, they were honored with the planting of the trees in their name in what's known as the Alley of the Just. And you have here, my mother and here are some of the other ladies that they saved, at the actual planting of the tree. Here is Don Giovanni Semioni. And here is, again, together with Don Angelo De La Torre, who later helped him in the north of Italy, Treviso. And the picture at the top shows the two trees blooming in Jerusalem. As a matter of fact, the last written communication I got from Don Giovanni before he-- he died is a postcard he wrote on a trip to Jerusalem. A postcard he wrote, and he said that the tree is growing beautifully. Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: These two pictures were taken during a visit by the priests. We invited them to come to Israel, and you see them here. Don Giovanni Semioni, Don Angelo De La Torre. Don-- Don Giovanni, again. Here he is with my mother and Mrs. [? Glatt, ?] another one of those women that he saved. And here, again. This was taken in Jerusalem. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is another picture taken during the priests' visit, when we invited them to come to Israel. They're now standing in the ruins of called Capernaum in Latin, Kfar Nahum in Hebrew, which is the actual synagogue on the shores of the Sea of Galilee, where Jesus was said to have preached. And on the left, this was taken several years later. On one of my visits again to Italy, I looked them both up. That's me in the middle with Don Giovanni on this side and Don Angelo De La Torre. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this picture was taken in New York when we invited Don Giovanni to come and visit us. It was in the early '70s. It's us, taken, I believe, right on 57th Street in Manhattan. Roosters.

speaker: interviewee
text: We have speed. What you have here is my son David. We have all these nicknames for-- for-- for him. He's now 18. He is at Rhode Island University. Now being 1995. Should I move this up so you can--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, I wanted to see the nickname, also.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, do you? Do you not?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is my-- he is the youngest of the three. This is my daughter, Adina. She is the middle one. She's now 21 going on 22, and she is at Emerson College in Boston. I don't have a recent one of my oldest child, a daughter called Daniela. She is a full-fledged lawyer, graduated from Washington University. And right now she's traveling all over the place, so I don't have anything recent. But I can show you a family picture. Do you want to cut while I look for it, or shall I find it?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, it's somewhere here. Here we go. All right, this was taken in Israel. Are you framed?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Down on the left. That's good. Perfect.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's fine? OK. This is our cousin, Debbie, who lives in Haifa. My wife, Adina again, David, and me. Taken in Haifa.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll show you that in 1993, I went to-- to Italy on the occasion of a book I had written. But what's interesting here, I don't know if you can see this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one? Oh, there. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: In fact, I'm looking at the barbed wire now for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. You want the whole thing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Tell me when I can talk.

speaker: interviewee
text: What you're looking at is from inside the Zecca Garrison in Florence, where we were kept after the big raid. And that's the wall that I jumped with the barbed wire right on top. By special permission, they allowed us to go in-- inside. And what you have here is Willy on the left, on my left, on the right in this picture. Me in the center. And this is Sigi. Sigi, who was deported from this place to Auschwitz with my father. So what you have here are the two youngsters who were actually inside of the Garrison. Willy, who never was because he succeeded in escaping from the movie theater. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that's me. Am I mixing you up?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, not at all. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me on the outside of the wall. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] There's another interesting picture if you have time.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. You're here in the courtyard of the Great Synagogue of Florence. And on the wall are the names of the Italian Jews of Florence who were deported and-- and killed. There are no foreign Jews there, because they didn't have the names. They didn't have anything. And what you have here is the honor guard of the city of Florence in traditional Renaissance costume. And the Bersaglieri, Italian guard standing honor here. And the rabbi here saying the prayers. This was taken recently, on the 50th anniversary of that raid that I spoke about in my tape, November 1943. This was taken November 1993, 50 years later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you mention the wall again, please?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, the upper wall with the names.

speaker: interviewee
text: The upper wall? This one here?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. If you want to-- I don't know, I don't want to. This is a closer shot of that wall you saw previously in the courtyard of the Synagogue of Florence, bearing the names of those Florentine Italian Jews deported and-- and killed. And in front of it, are the Three Musketeers again, with me in the center. Sigi on the right-hand side of the picture, Willy on the left. Incidentally, I never mentioned, that Willy and Sigi are brothers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I do it now. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I wanted to get a shot to the books. That's fantastic.

words.text: to get a shot to the

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't have to record-- do you want it against the black?

speaker: interviewee
text: You're looking at the story I have written as a tribute to what the priests did, and was published in-- in Italy. The original English title is Translated in Italian,

speaker: interviewee
text: They insist I show you this. This is a book of photographs that was published, I believe 1986, '87 on my work as a photographer in the movies, in the motion picture industry. The only connection is that I'm a photographer today-- I mean, the only connection to the tape of the interview, is that I'm a photographer today because way back in 1943, I happened to have been lucky enough to be hidden by a couple, both of them photographers, in the south of France. So one is the outgrowth of the other, a-- a continuing thread, if you want.
